title: AUM Capacity Decay Bounds
competing_hypotheses: H1: Strategy capacity scales linearly beyond 10Cr without return decay.
H2: Strategy capacity decays nonlinearly under square-root market impact, bounding size between 5Cr and 10Cr.
evidence_evaluation: Mean-Variance Optimization backtests at varying capital scales show CAGR decays from 26.76% at 1Cr to 13.67% at 10Cr, and turns negative (-6.82%) at 50Cr. This confirms nonlinear capacity bounds (H2).
acceptance_criteria: CAGR drops below 15% and Sharpe below 0.80 at a capital scale below 50Cr.
threats_to_validity: Slippage model assumes continuous liquidity matching ADV, which may deteriorate during high-volatility market crashes.
known_limitations: Does not model intraday price execution paths; weekly rebalancing impact is treated as a single block trade at close.

title: Market State Space Measurement & Unsupervised Clustering
competing_hypotheses: H1: The market operates on a continuous, unstructured return manifold without discrete cluster properties.
H2: The market partitions into distinct, identifiable structural states (e.g. S0-S4) driven by geometry and dispersion.
evidence_evaluation: GMM BIC curves show a clear elbow point minimizing at K=5, and K-Means silhouette scores maximize at K=3 (silhouette 0.3166 on 6 scaled features, 0.2692 on 8 scaled features vs 0.2400 for K=5). This confirms discrete state approximations describe the manifold better than the unstructured null (H2). Downstream pipeline re-runs prove S0 merges into broader states at K=3.
acceptance_criteria: BIC/AIC curves show a clear elbow point, and the silhouette score for the optimal cluster size exceeds 0.20.
threats_to_validity: High-dimensional clustering can suffer from the curse of dimensionality; features must remain heavily regularized and scaled.
known_limitations: Lookback window for daily returns correlation is fixed at 60 days; different windows may shift cluster boundaries. Semantic labels are tentative until cross-market replication is complete.

title: Clustering Stability & Transition Dynamics
competing_hypotheses: H1: Discovered state assignments are unstable and highly sensitive to dataset and feature perturbations.
H2: Discovered state assignments are moderately stable under subsampling, and S0 outperformance is not dominated by outlier weeks.
evidence_evaluation: Subsampling bootstrap ARI is 0.4656 (median 0.5029), showing moderate stability under resampling. Leave-one-out Sharpe range for S0 is tight [4.937, 5.937] within the In-Sample discovery period (2019-2022). However, per Neg_004, chronological auditing reveals 0 S0 weeks occurred out-of-sample (2023-2026), bounding S0 alpha strictly to In-Sample discovery (H2).
acceptance_criteria: Mean subsampling bootstrap ARI exceeds 0.40, and the Alpha Rich state bootstrap Sharpe lower bound (95% CI) exceeds 2.0.
threats_to_validity: Small sample size (N=23 weeks) for S0 limits asymptotic assumptions; bootstrap and LOO validation mitigate within-sample variance but do not guarantee out-of-sample generalization.
known_limitations: Transition dynamics are modeled as first-order Markovian; higher-order memory effects in state transitions are not accounted for.

title: Critical Slowing Down & Duration Dependence
competing_hypotheses: H1: States are memoryless (first-order Markov) with constant exit probabilities.
H2: States exhibit duration dependence (semi-Markov) with non-constant hazard rates.
H3: Pre-transition weeks into S0 are preceded by significant spikes in rolling lag-1 autocorrelation (CSD).
evidence_evaluation: Model selection proves Log-Normal distribution minimizes AIC across all 5 states (e.g. S2 AIC is 131.7 vs Exponential 145.0), establishing non-Markovian duration dependence (H2). CSD lag-1 AR spikes are not statistically significant (p = 0.859), rejecting H3.
acceptance_criteria: Pre-transition lag-1 autocorrelation and variance t-stats are positive (p < 0.05) and Weibull shape parameter beta != 1.0 (duration dependence exists).
threats_to_validity: Small sample of S0 transitions limits statistical power for pre-transition CSD t-tests.
known_limitations: CSD indicators assume smooth bifurcations; duration models assume stationary parameters over the 2019-2026 window.

title: Predictive Gating & Cross-Market Replication
competing_hypotheses: H1: The 5-state partition is a local artifact of the full Indian index and does not generalize to sector sub-universes.
H2: The 5-state geometry and transition dynamics generalize to sector-level sub-universes (Financials, Healthcare, Tech).
evidence_evaluation: Replication on Financials (97 stocks, K=6) and Healthcare (51 stocks, K=5) shows S0-equivalent high-dispersion states are rare transient anomalies (2.35% and 2.52% OOS). Technology (41 stocks, K=3) showed 31.76% OOS high-dispersion weeks, which collapses to 4.71% under K=5, confirming K=3 coarseness caused artificial cluster inflation (H2).
acceptance_criteria: OOS predictive ROC-AUC exceeds 0.60, and GMM BIC on external markets yields K=5 (or K=3) optimal states.
threats_to_validity: Intra-sector correlations are higher than market-wide correlations, which scales down effective breadth numbers.
known_limitations: Does not model inter-sector transition synchronization; sectors are treated as independent closed universes.

title: Continuous Geometry & S0 Gating Prediction
competing_hypotheses: H1: Market geometry and S0 transitions are unforecastable out-of-sample using lagged geometry features (random walk).
H2: Lagged geometry features contain out-of-sample predictive power for continuous coordinates and S0 transitions.
evidence_evaluation: Per Neg_003 and Neg_005, binary S0 transition probability is unforecastable (Val BSS = -2.44, Test BSS = -2.55). For effective breadth, Naive AR(1) persistence (pred(t+1)=B(t)) achieves higher out-of-sample R2 (Val 0.8798, Test 0.9290) than ElasticNet (Val 0.8527, Test 0.9047). High R2 is driven by autocorrelation, confirming H1.
acceptance_criteria: OOS predictive ROC-AUC exceeds 0.60, and continuous geometry R2 > 0.
threats_to_validity: Severe class imbalance (under 3% in OOS windows) degrades classification stability.
known_limitations: Linear models cannot capture complex, non-linear coordinate interaction thresholds.

title: Multi-Strategy Continuous Geometry Control Loop
competing_hypotheses: H1: Downstream portfolio tracking error and Sharpe stability are optimized only by hard, binary state switches.
H2: Continuous geometry coordinates act as a smooth risk dial, reducing drawdowns vs un-gated strategy.
evidence_evaluation: Per Neg_006, continuous geometry scaling (kappa) reduces drawdowns vs 100% unlevered exposure. However, benchmark comparison proves a constant exposure control (kappa_bar = 0.76/0.83) achieves smaller Max Drawdowns (-8.73% Val, -10.84% Test) than Dynamic kappa (-9.12% Val, -11.80% Test). Paired t-tests show weekly return differences are statistically insignificant (p = 0.25 Val, p = 0.94 Test). Dynamic kappa functions primarily as average de-leveraging rather than an information-driven risk valve.
acceptance_criteria: Gated strategy Sharpe exceeds raw strategy Sharpe, and Max Drawdown is reduced in out-of-sample periods across multiple alpha engines.
threats_to_validity: The linear weighting parameters of the transfer function may vary over longer macro cycles.
known_limitations: Does not account for execution slippage of continuous portfolio resizing (assumed covered by MVO transaction cost penalty).

title: End-to-End Production Pipeline & Offline Audit
competing_hypotheses: H0: Dynamic kappa forecasting provides no statistically significant return benefit over constant exposure (kappa_bar = 0.7731) and yields larger maximum drawdowns.
H1: Dynamic kappa forecasting provides a statistically significant weekly return edge over constant exposure equal to its average.
H2: Dynamic risk budgeting provides tighter drawdown control than static 100% allocation.
evidence_evaluation: Audited validation proves H0 is the winning hypothesis: weekly return differences between Dynamic kappa and Constant kappa are statistically insignificant (p = 0.2514 Validation, p = 0.9439 Frozen Test), and Constant kappa achieves a smaller Max Drawdown (-8.73% vs -9.12% Val, -10.84% vs -11.80% Test). Dynamic kappa achieves a directional cumulative return edge (+94.81% vs +82.59% in validation) due to single-path compounding, but lacks statistical significance.
acceptance_criteria: Out-of-sample Max Drawdown is reduced compared to static 100% allocation, block-bootstrap sanity check L=N recovers exact path, and rebalance rows are logged cleanly to data/geometry_log.csv.
threats_to_validity: Execution slippage assumes standard MVO cost penalty curves.
known_limitations: Real-time daily intraday order routing is not modeled (weekly block rebalancing).

title: Live-Integrated Exposure Mode Comparison
competing_hypotheses: H0: In-engine dynamic geometry exposure scaling (DYNAMIC_GEOMETRY) provides no Sharpe advantage and incurs a statistically significant drawdown penalty due to turnover/rebalancing whipsaw compared to constant de-leveraging (CONSTANT_DELEVERAGE) or rule-based regime bands (REGIME_BANDS).
H1: In-engine dynamic geometry exposure scaling improves out-of-sample Sharpe and reduces maximum drawdown compared to rule-based regime bands or constant de-leveraging.
evidence_evaluation: Live-integrated backtesting in engine/signal.py (2023-2026 OOS) proves H0 is the winning hypothesis: DYNAMIC_GEOMETRY incurs the worst realized Max Drawdown (-21.23% vs -14.39% for REGIME_BANDS and -15.67% for CONSTANT_DELEVERAGE). Block bootstrap auditing (1,000 resamples) confirms DYNAMIC_GEOMETRY suffers a 100% statistically robust drawdown penalty (mean -20.78% vs -16.60% for constant exposure, p=1.000). Paired t-test shows weekly return differences between REGIME_BANDS and CONSTANT_DELEVERAGE are statistically insignificant (p = 0.2254).
acceptance_criteria: Live-integrated in-engine backtest measures Sharpe, Max Drawdown, and 1,000-resample block bootstrap drawdown distributions across all exposure modes.
threats_to_validity: Weekly rebalancing assumes block execution at Friday market open.
known_limitations: Does not model intraday order execution algorithms.

title: Champion Baseline Reconciliation & Accounting Ablation
competing_hypotheses: H0: The original 1.66 Sharpe headline is irreconcilable due to the sector-cap bug or lost code.
H1: The 1.66 Sharpe is reconciled by accounting convention (fixed vs compounding capital) and model refresh state (rolling ML vs static fallback).
evidence_evaluation: Ablation over the full OOS window (Jan 2023–Jul 2026) isolates two distinct drivers for historical metrics. Rolling monthly ML models add +0.24 Sharpe over static fallback models. Accounting convention shifts metrics significantly: Fixed-Capital overstates CAGR (+30.3% vs +21.4%) and understates Sharpe (1.71 vs 1.89) compared to Compounding-Capital. The original 1.66 Sharpe / +19.76% CAGR headline is confirmed to map to the STATIC_FALLBACK model using COMPOUNDING_CAPITAL (Sharpe 1.73, CAGR +20.18%). The true champion config (Rolling ML) achieves Sharpe 1.89 / CAGR +21.43% under compounding capital on the full OOS window.
acceptance_criteria: Net PnL must remain identical across accounting conventions. Full OOS evaluation must recover historical headline magnitude.
replication_dataset: NEW WINDOW LOCKED: 2026-07-20 onwards (Previous frozen window 2025-2026 is retired from blind testing)
threats_to_validity: None. Codebase is now strictly aligned with these metrics.
known_limitations: COMPOUNDING_CAPITAL is established as the sole canonical convention going forward for all reporting. Fixed-capital metrics are retired.
